main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Diffusion Policy Policy Optimization (DPPO): a framework for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies using policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning","Carefully chosen design decisions for stable and robust fine-tuning of diffusion-based policies","Ablation studies to understand the mechanisms behind DPPO's performance"],"problems":["Inefficiency and instability of policy gradient methods for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies in continuous control and robot learning tasks","Suboptimal performance of behavior cloning due to limited or suboptimal expert data","Difficulty in fine-tuning pre-trained diffusion policies with reinforcement learning, especially in challenging robotic tasks with pixel observations and sparse rewards"],"datasets":["Simulated robotic tasks with pixel observations (specific environments not named in the provided text)","Real robot hardware deployment in a long-horizon, multi-stage manipulation task"],"metrics":["Training stability","Final task performance (e.g., success rate, reward)","Policy robustness to perturbation"],"results":[{"metric":"Training stability","value":"DPPO shows strong training stability across tasks compared to alternatives"},{"metric":"Final performance","value":"DPPO achieves marked improvements in final performance in challenging robotic tasks compared to off-policy Q-learning, weighted regression, and other demo-augmented RL methods"},{"metric":"Policy robustness","value":"DPPO finds policies that exhibit greater robustness to perturbation and transfer better to real-world hardware"}],"novelty_claims":["DPPO is the first generic framework and set of best practices for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies using policy gradient methods in RL for robot learning.","Contrary to prior belief, policy gradient methods can be highly effective for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies when using the DPPO framework.","DPPO leverages unique synergies between RL fine-tuning and diffusion parameterization, resulting in structured and on-manifold exploration, stable training, and robust policies.","DPPO demonstrates strong performance in both simulated and real-world robotic tasks, including zero-shot transfer from simulation to hardware."]}

methods: Diffusion Policy Policy Optimization (DPPO): a framework for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies using policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning
Carefully chosen design decisions for stable and robust fine-tuning of diffusion-based policies
Ablation studies to understand the mechanisms behind DPPO's performance
problems: Inefficiency and instability of policy gradient methods for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies in continuous control and robot learning tasks
Suboptimal performance of behavior cloning due to limited or suboptimal expert data
Difficulty in fine-tuning pre-trained diffusion policies with reinforcement learning, especially in challenging robotic tasks with pixel observations and sparse rewards
datasets: Simulated robotic tasks with pixel observations (specific environments not named in the provided text)
Real robot hardware deployment in a long-horizon, multi-stage manipulation task
metrics: Final task performance (e.g., success rate, reward)

results.value: DPPO shows strong training stability across tasks compared to alternatives
DPPO achieves marked improvements in final performance in challenging robotic tasks compared to off-policy Q-learning, weighted regression, and other demo-augmented RL methods
DPPO finds policies that exhibit greater robustness to perturbation and transfer better to real-world hardware

novelty_claims: DPPO is the first generic framework and set of best practices for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies using policy gradient methods in RL for robot learning.
Contrary to prior belief, policy gradient methods can be highly effective for fine-tuning diffusion-based policies when using the DPPO framework.
DPPO leverages unique synergies between RL fine-tuning and diffusion parameterization, resulting in structured and on-manifold exploration, stable training, and robust policies.
DPPO demonstrates strong performance in both simulated and real-world robotic tasks, including zero-shot transfer from simulation to hardware.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Efficient Diffusion Policy (EDP)","Action approximation for diffusion models","Use of DPM-Solver (ODE-based sampler) for faster sampling","Approximate policy likelihood using evidence lower bound from DDPM","Plugging EDP into various offline RL algorithms (TD3+BC, CRR, IQL)"],"problems":["Computational inefficiency of diffusion-based policies in offline RL due to long Markov chains for sampling","Incompatibility of diffusion policies with maximum likelihood-based RL algorithms due to intractable likelihoods","Need for more expressive policy parameterizations in offline RL, especially for complex/multi-modal data distributions"],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark (including gym-locomotion and three other domains)"],"metrics":["Training time","Task performance (normalized score on D4RL tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Training time (gym-locomotion tasks)","value":"Reduced from 5 days (Diffusion-QL) to 5 hours (EDP)"},{"metric":"Performance (D4RL domains)","value":"EDP achieves new state-of-the-art on all four D4RL domains, outperforming previous methods by large margins"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes EDP, a diffusion policy that avoids running the full sampling chain during training via action approximation, greatly improving efficiency","EDP is compatible with likelihood-based RL algorithms by approximating policy likelihood, unlike previous diffusion policies","EDP achieves significant speed-up (up to 24x) in training time without performance loss","EDP sets new state-of-the-art results on all four D4RL domains when plugged into various offline RL algorithms"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Diffusion Q-learning (Diffusion-QL): representing the policy as a conditional diffusion model (denoising diffusion probabilistic model, DDPM)","Learning an action-value function (Q-function) and incorporating a policy improvement term into the diffusion model's training loss","Policy regularization via sample-based distribution matching using diffusion models"],"problems":["Offline reinforcement learning: learning optimal policies from static, previously collected datasets without environment interaction","Poor performance of standard RL methods in offline settings due to function approximation errors on out-of-distribution actions","Limited expressiveness of policy classes (e.g., Gaussian policies) leading to suboptimal solutions and poor policy regularization","Inadequate regularization methods (e.g., KL divergence, MMD) for offline RL"],"datasets":["D4RL benchmark tasks","Simple 2D bandit example with a multimodal behavior policy"],"metrics":["Task-specific performance metrics on D4RL (e.g., normalized score)","Comparison to state-of-the-art baselines"],"results":[{"metric":"D4RL benchmark performance","value":"Diffusion-QL achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of D4RL tasks"},{"metric":"2D bandit example","value":"Diffusion-QL outperforms prior methods in settings with multimodal behavior policies"}],"novelty_claims":["First to use diffusion models (DDPMs) as expressive policy classes for offline RL, enabling better modeling of complex, multimodal behavior policies","Proposes a new loss that combines behavior cloning and Q-learning guidance within the diffusion model framework","Demonstrates that diffusion models provide a powerful sample-based policy regularization method without the need for explicit density estimation or extra behavior cloning","Shows that coupling the expressiveness of diffusion models with Q-learning guidance leads to superior offline RL performance compared to prior methods"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Imitation Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (IBRL): trains a separate imitation learning (IL) policy on demonstrations, then uses the IL policy to propose alternative actions for both online exploration and bootstrapping target values in RL; during online interaction, the agent chooses between IL and RL actions based on Q-value; during training, Q-value updates bootstrap from the better action (IL or RL); pre-fills RL replay buffer with demonstrations"],"problems":["Sample inefficiency of RL in robotic control tasks, especially with sparse rewards","High cost and limited generalization of collecting comprehensive expert demonstrations for IL","Distribution shift issues in policies learned from static datasets","Balancing RL and IL losses and architectures in prior methods","Need for efficient and robust learning in real-world robotics with limited data"],"datasets":["6 simulation robotics tasks (not named in the excerpt)","3 real-world robotics tasks (including a deformable cloth hanging task)"],"metrics":["Task success rate (0/1 sparse reward)","Relative performance improvement over baseline methods"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance improvement in hardest simulation task","value":"IBRL nearly doubles the performance over the second best method"},{"metric":"Performance improvement in real-world deformable cloth hanging task","value":"IBRL performs 2.4x better than the second best RL method"},{"metric":"Performance in real-world tasks after 2 hours of training","value":"Prior methods unable to surpass BC baseline; IBRL significantly outperforms"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes IBRL, a new framework that explicitly integrates a separate IL policy into both the action selection and value bootstrapping phases of RL, rather than just pretraining or regularizing RL with IL.","Allows the use of deeper, more powerful IL networks than typically feasible in RL, by keeping IL and RL policies separate.","Eliminates the need for explicit regularization loss and hyperparameter tuning to balance RL and IL, simplifying training.","Demonstrates significant improvements in sample efficiency and performance, especially in harder tasks, over prior methods."]}

methods: Imitation Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (IBRL): trains a separate imitation learning (IL) policy on demonstrations, then uses the IL policy to propose alternative actions for both online exploration and bootstrapping target values in RL; during online interaction, the agent chooses between IL and RL actions based on Q-value; during training, Q-value updates bootstrap from the better action (IL or RL); pre-fills RL replay buffer with demonstrations

results.metric: Performance improvement in real-world deformable cloth hanging task

novelty_claims: Proposes IBRL, a new framework that explicitly integrates a separate IL policy into both the action selection and value bootstrapping phases of RL, rather than just pretraining or regularizing RL with IL.
Allows the use of deeper, more powerful IL networks than typically feasible in RL, by keeping IL and RL policies separate.
Eliminates the need for explicit regularization loss and hyperparameter tuning to balance RL and IL, simplifying training.
Demonstrates significant improvements in sample efficiency and performance, especially in harder tasks, over prior methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Policy-Guided Diffusion (PGD): using diffusion models to generate entire trajectories under the behavior distribution, with guidance from the target policy to move locomotion tasks (Todorov et al., 2012)","Maze2d navigation environments"],"metrics":["Offline RL performance (normalized score or return)","Dynamics error (model error in synthetic methods: Policy-Guided Diffusion (PGD): using diffusion models to generate entire trajectories under the behavior distribution, with guidance from the target policy to move synthetic experience further on-policy
Training a trajectory diffusion model on offline data and applying target policy guidance at each diffusion step to increase the likelihood of sampled actions under the target policy
Using synthetic experience generated by PGD as a drop-in substitute for real data in offline RL algorithms
problems: Distribution shift between behavior policy and target policy in offline reinforcement learning, leading to instability and overestimation bias
Compounding model error in autoregressive world models for synthetic data generation, requiring truncated rollouts
Out-of-sample generalization issues due to underrepresentation of target policy state-action pairs in offline datasets
datasets: D4RL (Fu et al., 2020) datasets
MuJoCo locomotion tasks (Todorov et al., 2012)
Maze2d navigation environments
metrics: Offline RL performance (normalized score or return)
Dynamics error (model error in synthetic trajectories)
Target policy likelihood (likelihood of synthetic actions under the target policy)

results.metric: TD3+BC performance improvement (MuJoCo locomotion tasks, aggregated)
results.value: 11.2% improvement over training on real data
PGD achieves lower dynamics error than PETS
PGD matches the target policy likelihood of PETS

novelty_claims: Proposes policy-guided diffusion (PGD), a method that uses diffusion models to generate entire synthetic trajectories with guidance from the target policy, as an alternative to autoregressive world models in offline RL
PGD models a regularized target distribution that balances action likelihood under both the target and behavior policies, resulting in plausible and on-policy synthetic trajectories
Demonstrates that PGD can serve as an effective drop-in replacement for real data in offline RL, improving performance across standard algorithms and environments
Shows that PGD avoids compounding model error by generating full trajectories, unlike prior model-based approaches that require truncated rollouts

Theoretically proves that CPQL achieves policy improvement with accurate guidance for offline RL.
Introduces an empirical loss to stabilize consistency policy training, replacing the standard consistency loss.
Demonstrates seamless extension of CPQL to online RL tasks without additional exploration strategies.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on a large number of offline and online RL tasks, with significant speed improvements over previous diffusion-based methods.

methods: Family Offline-to-Online RL (FamO2O) framework
Universal model for training a family of policies with varying improvement/constraint intensities
Balance model that maps states to balance coefficients for state-adaptive policy selection

novelty_claims: FamO2O is the first offline-to-online RL approach to use state-adaptive improvement-constraint balances for each state.
The framework trains a family of policies and uses a balance model to select the appropriate policy per state, rather than using a single global trade-off.
Theoretical proof that point-wise (state-adaptive) KL constraints provide a higher policy performance upper bound than distributional KL constraints used in prior works.
FamO2O achieves statistically significant improvements and state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.

Open-sourcing of datasets and algorithm implementations to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison in the field
Demonstration that history-dependent models are particularly effective for learning from human demonstration data, while batch RL algorithms struggle

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["JUICER pipeline: combines expressive policy architectures (diffusion models), dataset expansion via data noising and simulation-based augmentation, and iterative model development cycles (collect-and-infer)","Policy representation as conditional diffusion models","Prediction of action chunks (multiple future actions)","Structured data augmentation and noising around bottleneck states using simulation resets and synthetic corrective actions","Automatic dataset expansion by adding successful rollouts from model evaluation"],"problems":["Data-efficient imitation learning for robotic assembly with a small number of demonstrations","Learning high-precision, long-horizon visuomotor policies from raw images","Improving robustness around bottleneck regions in assembly tasks","Reducing the demonstration data collection burden for complex manipulation tasks"],"datasets":["Four simulated furniture assembly tasks from the FurnitureBench benchmark"],"metrics":["Task success rate"],"results":[{"metric":"Task success rate","value":"JUICER outperforms imitation and data augmentation baselines on all four FurnitureBench tasks"},{"metric":"Task success rate with 10 demonstrations","value":"High-performance policies achieved with as few as 10 human demonstrations"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes JUICER, a pipeline that enables high-precision, long-horizon robotic assembly from a small number of demonstrations","Combines diffusion policy architectures with structured data augmentation and iterative dataset expansion for data-efficient imitation learning","Introduces a bottleneck state labeling and trajectory augmentation tool for generating synthetic corrective actions in simulation","Demonstrates that each component of the pipeline improves over vanilla imitation learning baselines","Shows that high-performance policies can be learned with as few as 10 demonstrations"]}

methods: JUICER pipeline: combines expressive policy architectures (diffusion models), dataset expansion via data noising and simulation-based augmentation, and iterative model development cycles (collect-and-infer)
Policy representation as conditional diffusion models
Prediction of action chunks (multiple future actions)
Structured data augmentation and noising around bottleneck states using simulation resets and synthetic corrective actions
Automatic dataset expansion by adding successful rollouts from model evaluation
problems: Data-efficient imitation learning for robotic assembly with a small number of demonstrations
Learning high-precision, long-horizon visuomotor policies from raw images
Improving robustness around bottleneck regions in assembly tasks
Reducing the demonstration data collection burden for complex manipulation tasks
datasets: Four simulated furniture assembly tasks from the FurnitureBench benchmark

results.value: JUICER outperforms imitation and data augmentation baselines on all four FurnitureBench tasks
High-performance policies achieved with as few as 10 human demonstrations

novelty_claims: Proposes JUICER, a pipeline that enables high-precision, long-horizon robotic assembly from a small number of demonstrations
Combines diffusion policy architectures with structured data augmentation and iterative dataset expansion for data-efficient imitation learning
Introduces a bottleneck state labeling and trajectory augmentation tool for generating synthetic corrective actions in simulation
Demonstrates that each component of the pipeline improves over vanilla imitation learning baselines
Shows that high-performance policies can be learned with as few as 10 demonstrations
